exemplar.title: To what extent the spatial management of the Bystrzyca river facilitates lives of Lublin inhabitants?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent the spatial management of the Bystrzyca river facilitates lives of Lublin inhabitants?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Clear, accurate and effective communication of the investigationâs focus throughout the report
Wellâstructured methodology and use of sub-headings that keep the inquiry aligned to the research question
weaknesses: Research question remains somewhat abstract (âfacilitates livesâ) and would benefit from more precise, measurable indicators
Key methodological details (sampling bias justification, data analysis techniques) are under-developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The candidate sustains an explicit, purposeful focus and communicates the topic with clarity (aligning with 5â6 band), but the research questionâs abstraction and some methodological omissions prevent full meeting of the 6-mark standard. Averaging strong topic clarity (band 5â6) with solid yet partial question focus and methodology (both band 3â4) yields a final mark of 5/6.

strengths: Sound knowledge of river geography, urban planning concepts and local context with appropriate use of secondary sources
Mostly accurate and consistent use of geographical terminology in context
weaknesses: Theoretical models are treated descriptively rather than critically applied (e.g., urban landâuse, carrying capacity)
Some factual statements (e.g., cyanobacteria impacts) lack quantitative backing from environmental data sources
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates clear subject knowledge and generally correct terminology (band 3â4) but falls short of âexcellentâ conceptual depth and consistent use of theoretical models required for the 5â6 band, warranting 4/6.

strengths: Good integration of primary and secondary dataâmaps, graphs and questionnaire results are clearly connected to the research question
Analysis moves beyond description by interpreting spatial patterns and proposing management solutions
weaknesses: Argument and discussion are unevenâsections drift into descriptive inventories without critical weighing or counter-argument
Evaluation of data limitations (e.g., water-quality, survey bias) is acknowledged but lacks depth and concrete testing of causeâeffect relationships
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands fall within the 4â6 band: research application is good and relevant, analysis is solid, and argument coherent but uneven. Averaging these mid-band performances leads to a total of 5/12 for Critical Thinking.

strengths: Layout conventions (formatting, page numbering, captions, legends) are correctly applied, enhancing readability
Report structure (title page, contents, numbered sections) supports clear navigation
weaknesses: A few composite figures are overly crowded and hinder clarity
Some captions are verbose and could be more concise
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation consistently meets the descriptors of the top band (3â4): structure is clearly appropriate and layout considerations are correctly applied, justifying the full 4/4 marks.

strandSelections.reasoning: Primary and secondary data are brought together in maps, graphs and photographic evidence to address the research question.  The student **integrates questionnaire results, field observations and literature** to explain recreational, ecological and infrastructural functions of the river corridor.  While some links between data and conclusions are descriptive, the majority of evidence is clearly connected to the research issue, showing good relevance.  Occasional gaps (e.g., limited triangulation of survey bias) prevent attainment of the highest band, but overall research application meets the âgoodâ standard (4â6 band), meriting a mid-range score of 5.

The methodology section identifies two complementary strands of primary research (44 km field transect with photographic inventory; 116-response online questionnaire) and a range of secondary sources (municipal documents, academic reports, websites).  The choice of mixed methods is **generally appropriate** to the research question and is described with reasonable clarity (dates, distances, sampling period).  However, key detailsâsuch as justification of questionnaire design, consideration of sampling bias and explanation of data analysis techniquesâremain under-developed.  The range of secondary sources is adequate but not demonstrably âwide and consistently appropriateâ.  These features align best with the 3â4 band, showing a mostly complete though not fully rigorous methodological design.

The student displays **sound knowledge of river geography, urban planning concepts and local context**: detailed physical description of the riverâs geomorphology, discussion of green-blue infrastructure, and references to urban revitalisation theory.  Secondary sources are generally used to support factual statements, and original maps/photos reinforce understanding.  Nonetheless, treatment of theoretical models (e.g., urban land-use, recreational carrying capacity) is limited, and some explanations remain largely descriptive.  The command of content is therefore clear but not yet âexcellentâ, positioning the work securely in the 3â4 band.

Layout conventionsâmargins, font consistency, page numbering, word count, and extensive use of labelled figures/tablesâare all present and largely correct.  Maps include titles, legends and north arrows; tables and charts are captioned.  Some images are crowded, and a few captions are verbose, but **overall visual presentation is clear and functional**, justifying placement in the upper band (3â4).

Geographical terminology such as âgreen-blue infrastructureâ, âretention reservoirâ, âriparian vegetationâ, âspatial frameworkâ and âfamily allotment gardensâ is employed **mostly accurately and in context**, supporting the narrative.  Occasional language lapses (e.g., âfacilitates livesâ, âfishermen menâ) and some informal phrasing indicate minor inconsistencies, but they do not impede comprehension.  Conceptual use (e.g., linking spatial management to quality-of-life indicators) is evident though sometimes superficial.  This reflects the 3â4 band for generally accurate and appropriate but not consistently sophisticated use of terms and concepts.

The student introduces the investigation with a concise overview of the geographical context, clearly sign-posting the focus on âspatial management of the Bystrzyca Riverâ and its impact on Lublin residents.  The title, abstracted topic statement, maps and early background paragraphs all align to a single, unmistakable purpose.  Throughout the essay the reader is consistently reminded of this focus via sub-headings (e.g. â3.3 Field Workâ, â3.3.11 Problems and addressing the problemsâ) and repeated reference to how each data set links back to facilitation of residentsâ lives.  The sustained and explicit alignment between the stated topic and the material discussed demonstrates **accurate, purposeful communication and an unambiguous focus**, fulfilling the descriptors of the 5â6 band.

The essay develops a logical, chronological discussion (introduction â methods â analysis â conclusion) and often links back to the central question.  Arguments regarding facilitation of recreation, transport and cultural value are stated and supported with examples.  However, **some sections drift into descriptive inventories** of facilities without critically weighing their relative significance or integrating counter-arguments.  The conclusion summarises findings but stops short of a nuanced, sustained synthesis expected for the 7â9 band.  Overall coherence is good but undermined by episodic narrative and occasional repetition, aligning with the 4â6 band; a score of 4 reflects these uneven qualities.

The analysis moves beyond mere description by interpreting spatial patterns (e.g., identification of the âmost exploited areaâ), linking user perceptions to infrastructural distribution, and proposing management solutions.  Graphs are explained and connected to field evidence, and limitations are acknowledged in the Evaluation.  Nevertheless, some evaluations are **limited in depth**âfor instance, water-quality issues are discussed without quantitative support, and causeâeffect relationships are sometimes asserted rather than critically tested.  Conclusions are mostly supported but not comprehensively justified, fitting the middle of the 4â6 band.

The report follows the prescribed EE formatâtitle page, abstracted topic details, contents, numbered sections, bibliography and appendices.  Headings and sub-headings create a clear roadmap, and visual material is integrated with captions.  Minor structural inefficiencies (lengthy sub-sections, occasional duplication of figures) slightly impede flow, yet the overall organisation **supports reader understanding effectively**.  These features meet the description of the 3â4 band.

The research question â âTo what extent does the spatial management of the Bystrzyca river facilitate lives of Lublin inhabitants?â â is **clearly stated and guides the investigation**, but the key concept of âfacilitates livesâ remains somewhat abstract and is interpreted broadly (leisure, transport, ecology, culture, etc.).  While this breadth still allows a coherent enquiry, it dilutes the sharpness that a fully focused question at the 5â6 band would require.  Nevertheless, the wording is sufficiently narrow in geographic scope (Bystrzyca within Lublin) and in thematic intent (spatial management impacts) to merit solid credit within the 3â4 band for partial but evident focus.

annotations.comment: The research question uses the abstract term âfacilitates livesâ, which could lead to vague interpretations. Operationalize âfacilitates livesâ by defining specific indicators (e.g., transport access, recreational use, water quality) to sharpen focus and ensure measurable enquiry.
The detailed table of contents effectively maps each section and subsection, supporting navigation and reflecting a clear organizational structure.
The document correctly provides a word count at the beginning, meeting the formal requirement and demonstrating attention to formatting guidelines.
While incorporating Apple Watch elevation data is innovative, the report should clarify the accuracy and potential errors of this source when analyzing terrain influence on river accessibility.
Elevation change data is presented without linking analysis to facilitation outcomes; discuss how variations in elevation along the route affect resident access and facility use.
The discussion of the cityâs green-blue infrastructure potential is aspirational but lacks critical evaluation or comparison with case studies of successful river-based urban revitalization.
Winter swimming figure is informative but would benefit from cross-tabulation by demographic or motivational factors to explain the 5.2% participation rate.
Figure 15 combines multiple park facilities in one composite, but the density of images and labels hinders readability; splitting into focused sub-figures would improve clarity.
The analysis of questionnaire age and residence data remains largely descriptive; include basic statistical measures or tests to support claims about demographic trends.
Map 6 shows path networks under construction and planned, yet the analysis omits discussion of connectivity gaps and how these influence the river corridorâs functional integration.
The narrative extols canoeing as ideal for city dwellers, yet survey data show 66.4% never canoe; reconcile this contradiction by discussing barriers to participation.
The description of Arena Lublin is detailed, but lacks attendance or usage statistics to link facility presence with facilitation of resident activities.
The introduction opens with general river functions but lacks conciseness and direct linkage to the Bystrzyca case; refocus to justify why this river in Lublin merits specific academic investigation.
While the physical and ecological description of the riverâs geomorphology is thorough, it would benefit from citing specific theoretical models to demonstrate deeper conceptual engagement.
The candidate-drawn map is well-constructed with a clear legend, scale, and north arrow, showcasing strong cartographic presentation skills.
The fieldwork description specifies distance and duration but omits justification for using a cycling transect and doesnât address how this method may introduce sampling bias.
The questionnaire is reported to have 116 responses, but there is no discussion of sampling strategy, recruitment procedure, or how representative the sample is of the target population.
Four riverside parks are profiled, but survey item 7 amalgamates parks; consider a refined questionnaire to capture user preferences for each park individually.
The art exhibition section celebrates temporary installations, but lacks critical assessment of whether these events leave lasting spatial impacts or community engagement beyond the festival period.
Descriptions of cyanobacteria and water quality issues are not backed by quantitative water-quality data; integrate secondary environmental monitoring reports for validation.
The conclusion that local bus lines improve reservoir access is not directly supported by survey questions; consider gathering transport accessibility data or participant travel logs.
The fishing participation chart lists frequencies, but the analysis would be stronger if coupled with observational counts or distribution across river sections to validate survey self-reports.
Discussion of the dog park omits user-count data or survey feedback; incorporate usage observations or questionnaire items to assess its actual uptake and community value.
The claim that Bar pod PstrÄgiem is the only dining spot on the riverbank needs support from survey dataâcross-reference with respondentsâ dining preferences to substantiate this assertion.
The interpretation of bicycle path usage focuses on percentages but does not explore underlying reasons for low daily use; link this to accessibility or seasonal factors for deeper insight.
The integration of photographic evidence alongside survey charts in Figure 3 effectively grounds quantitative data in real-world context, enhancing interpretive strength.
Map 7 is a detailed spatial visualization of the field transect with marked stops (AâM), clearly supporting the sequential narrative of the methodology.
The use of numbered sub-sections (3.3.1, 3.3.2, etc.) throughout the field work chapter maintains clarity and guides the reader through the site-by-site analysis.
The nature trail description highlights conservation infrastructure but overlooks any monitoring outcomes; include data on bat box occupancy or species surveys to demonstrate ecological impact.
Map 9 designates the âmost exploited areaâ but does not explain the criteria or metrics used to identify this segment; clarify how exploitation was measured spatially and statistically.

The problems and solutions section proposes infrastructural interventions but lacks feasibility analysis or consideration of stakeholder constraints; discuss potential funding, maintenance, and governance challenges.
The conclusion reiterates findings but remains overly descriptive; restructure to synthesize key insights thematically and directly address each element of the research question.

The evaluation acknowledges sampling skew towards younger respondents but could be strengthened by outlining concrete measures (e.g., stratified sampling) to improve representativeness in future surveys.
